# Thread: Biohacking Diastasis Recti-visceral vs subcutaneous fat formation & CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ValerieBee — 2017-08-10T22:29:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221380>

Trying to understand why the body stores fat as visceral rather than subcutaneous. Is it a direct correlation with leptin resistance and inflammatory response? Jack mentions in various places that visceral fat is the most dangerous because it is the most inflammatory. Is it a self-perpetuating cycle where leptin resistance creates an inflammatory environment because the calories not used by the liver cannot be properly used, thus going back to the liver and being "placed into fat storage or stuck inside the liver cell because of high insulin levels." More fat, higher leptin.

I see that CT reduces visceral fat first--is this because it reduces IL-6? And I see some [debate around the internet that CT increases subcutaneous fat, attributed to a Jack blog](https://www.paleohacks.com/dr-jack-kruse/does-the-quilt-s-cold-therapy-protocol-increase-subcutaneous-fat-13788) and some unnamed studies, but I cannot find that blog? (I'd ask the poster on that site, but it is from 2012).

> He says on his blog that it does increase subcutaneous fat. And there are enough credible studies that also point toward an increase in subcutaneous fat. So I think it's probably safe to say, that it increases subcutaneous fat. I think it's also safe to say, based on credible studies, that it also increases brown fat, and increased brown fat activity has an adverse effect on white visceral fat... which is a good thing.

I'm looking to biohack my diastasis recti as part of my slim down and optimal lifestyle, and while I see people have results reducing visceral fat with CT, I really like to understand the why, and look toward prevention rather than just get some flashy results.

I'll start with face dunks, but I'm also inclined to ice pack my tummy directly... how does location on the body affect CT, if you are not simply doing total immersion?

### ValerieBee — 2017-08-10T22:43:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221382>

@[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) Wondering if this should be moved to the CT forum?

### Da-mo — 2017-08-11T00:23:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221388>

I know your questions are about CT effect but I think that you cant divorce the effects of CT on visceral/subcutaneous fat from the O6:O3 effects. They seem to go hand in hand.

If I remember correctly O6/O3 ratio has some effects regarding visceral versus subcutaneous fat.

And the CT protocol makes mention of cold urticaria indicating high tissue O6 and to first load up on O3 before CTing. Brain Freeze is apparently another sign of too high an O6:O3 ratio. JK even put this in red text in the CT easy start guide.

> **\*Note: If you develop cold urticaria at this time****, this is a sign you have high levels of tissue and serum omega six content. Stop the experiment and adjust your diet until you have a blood omega six to three ratio that is below 10 to 1 using a ketogenic paleolithic diet.**

From down in the comments [https://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/](https://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/)

> _Jack_ says:
> [March 25, 2012 at 10:45 am](https://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/#comment-34759)
> If youre at 2:1 and Cting I think your fine…..but you only need to be 4:1 2:1 is where marine mammals who live deep in the cold approach the 1:1 ratio…….when you live in deep cold your ratios then begin to become 03/6 extreme the other way……and this changes adipocyte biology as we understand it……..this is why water based mammals have blubber and land based ones only get it when it is warm………and shred it in cold land based situations……..its all evolution’s work fitting us into the environment and the cell membrane epigenetics is what controls the show.

From Brain Gut 12 also down in the comments [https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/)

> _Ben G_ says:
> [September 16, 2012 at 2:13 am](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/#comment-42878)
> Dr. Kruse,
> B/C sumo wrestlers eat a lot of sushi/seafood, they seem to have low visceral fat (and relatively healthier metabolic profile)? Is the Iodine + DHA the reason for it?
> What’s your position on eating kelp for iodine as well?
> [Reply](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/#comment-42878)
>
> -   Jack says:
>     [September 16, 2012 at 6:23 am](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-12-dare-to-disagree/#comment-42880)
>     @[BenG](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1088/) This has been mentioned in the literature but I have no idea if it is true. My position on kelp is made pretty clear in Brain Gut 6.

and this research . . . . [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/) Long-Chain Omega-3 Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids May Be Beneficial for Reducing Obesity—A Review

> The preferential reduction in the accumulation of visceral fat has been confirmed in two subsequent studies \[[9](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/#B9-nutrients-02-01212),[13](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257626/#B13-nutrients-02-01212)\] and may be mediated primarily by DHA

### caroline — 2017-08-11T02:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-diastasis-recti-visceral-vs-subcutaneous-fat-formation-ct.19991/post-221394>

Good thread Valerie ........ You ask great questions!

### ValerieBee — 2017-08-11T14:45:27+0300

# Thread: Overstimulation from Sperti Fiji
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overstimulation-from-sperti-fiji.19992/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### karenr — 2017-08-10T23:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/overstimulation-from-sperti-fiji.19992/post-221387>

So I've been using the Sperti Fiji (UVa and UVB) for about 5 days now, starting very slowly just a few minutes a day, and feeling very overstimulated. Normally I would just chalk that up to my dysautonomia and chronic sleep issues, and I'm at the very beginning of reconfiguring my system. When I was a kid, after a day at the beach I would feel all hyper too.

But a friend who's in good health tried my Sperti lamp for just 1.5 minutes, and said he couldn't sleep that night. Does this have to do with deficiency of early morning light to make melatonin? Does it make sense that UV exposure even at solar noon could be unbalancing without enough balanced red/blue beforehand?

At 43 latitude, we do what we can! I have the Solar Glo lizard light on order.

# Thread: Penmanship
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Stardust — 2017-08-11T07:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221403>

My 7 year old who was diagnosed with ADHD has troubled penmanship. She is a very good speller and can form sentences just fine. Getting is on paper is a struggle. It is hard to motivate her to write. I saw no improvement with occupational therapy. Does anyone have any recommendations?

### caroline — 2017-08-11T11:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221415>

maybe this is way off base - my daughter has dyslexia.

It seems that it takes many different forms. When my daughter reads - it is all just separate words to her with no connection.
some people see colours, for others the words just fall off the page.

A friend of ours has a son who had difficulty reading - so they gave him comic books. It made a huge difference when this boy could read something that was of interest. He is a really bright kid btw.

I understand that your daughter's problem is writing - but something is not connecting properly I would think

What is her diet like? Is she playing outside a lot?

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-11T13:25:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221423>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221403)
>
> My 7 year old who was diagnosed with ADHD has troubled penmanship. She is a very good speller and can form sentences just fine. Getting is on paper is a struggle. It is hard to motivate her to write. I saw no improvement with occupational therapy. Does anyone have any recommendations?

I taught my children that writing could be fun by getting them to write about what interested them ... And if they couldn't spell a word or just wanted to be creative (DD loves art), they could incorporate pictures or find a different way of expressing a word ... So a line of writing might include a picture of a spaceship, or a dinosaur or a princess .... If they were writing the word red, they could write the word itself in red ... (DS2 learned how to spell the colours from that.) My DD was a bit older so she would write the word circle as a circle, the words "upside down"....upside down  it became a game to find words and phrases that she could express in a different way... It was a way of giving them self confidence - if writing can be seen to be interesting, and fun, it became "easy" (and I think it helps form neural networks between different parts of the brain), it honours what they know rather than focusing on what they can't do, and it helped motivation.

### Stardust — 2017-08-11T22:42:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221478>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221415)
>
> maybe this is way off base - my daughter has dyslexia.
>
> It seems that it takes many different forms. When my daughter reads - it is all just separate words to her with no connection.
> some people see colours, for others the words just fall off the page.
>
> A friend of ours has a son who had difficulty reading - so they gave him comic books. It made a huge difference when this boy could read something that was of interest. He is a really bright kid btw.
>
> I understand that your daughter's problem is writing - but something is not connecting properly I would think
>
> What is her diet like? Is she playing outside a lot?

In the beginning, we thought it was Dyslexia. Well, the school thought it was dyslexia. We had her assessed and they determined she was not. I agree with the not connecting properly. Her motor skills in general are lacking. Her diet is 75% Epi-Paleo. My kids have had a more difficult time shifting over than I did. She does play outside most of the day. She actually dreads being indoors which is great. I'm going to sign her up for Piano class and see what other motor skill activities I can add. How old is your daughter? I was diagnosed with dyslexia as a child as well.

### Stardust — 2017-08-11T22:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221479>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=221423)
>
> I taught my children that writing could be fun by getting them to write about what interested them ... And if they couldn't spell a word or just wanted to be creative (DD loves art), they could incorporate pictures or find a different way of expressing a word ... So a line of writing might include a picture of a spaceship, or a dinosaur or a princess .... If they were writing the word red, they could write the word itself in red ... (DS2 learned how to spell the colours from that.) My DD was a bit older so she would write the word circle as a circle, the words "upside down"....upside down  it became a game to find words and phrases that she could express in a different way... It was a way of giving them self confidence - if writing can be seen to be interesting, and fun, it became "easy" (and I think it helps form neural networks between different parts of the brain), it honours what they know rather than focusing on what they can't do, and it helped motivation.

Those are some great strategies! I need to keep her interested in school. She is so bright! I do not want her to begin to dislike learning because writing is not fun for her. She lacks self-confidence in her writing ability. She is in 3rd grade this year and by next year she will have to be writing so much more.

### caroline — 2017-08-12T02:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221488>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221478)
>
> In the beginning, we thought it was Dyslexia. Well, the school thought it was dyslexia. We had her assessed and they determined she was not. I agree with the not connecting properly. Her motor skills in general are lacking. Her diet is 75% Epi-Paleo. My kids have had a more difficult time shifting over than I did. She does play outside most of the day. She actually dreads being indoors which is great. I'm going to sign her up for Piano class and see what other motor skill activities I can add. How old is your daughter? I was diagnosed with dyslexia as a child as well.

I am a grandmother .....my daughter loves cooking so she can read cookbooks and really connects with that.

Actually - do you cook with your daughter? maybe reading a recipe and writing out in simple ingredient points would interest her?

My younger daughter was a very difficult eater. We got her to chose a recipe and then help shop for stuff and then help make it.

That made a huge difference - she then had control over the process.

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-12T15:14:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221511>

Its been a bumpy road since DS2 was a young child, light sensitive, food phobic, water phobic, anxious, delayed speech development ... (turned out he is incredibly sensitive to the effects of fluoride). Watching his inner doctor guide him to recovery when it got the chance is one of my greatest joys. A lot of moments of grace. We chose the guitar over the piano ... DS2 was keen to learn electric guitar, the lessons were geared mainly around acoustic but with enough electric to keep him motivated. Its turned out that he is good at it, but its not his passion, even the electric guitar. For lessons, he would do just enough practice, but if he'd done more because he was passionate about it, he would be exceptional. He sometimes goes outside and sits under the trees with his acoustic guitar, and every time he does I have another moment of grace. .

### caroline — 2017-08-13T03:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221572>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221479)
>
> Those are some great strategies! I need to keep her interested in school. She is so bright! I do not want her to begin to dislike learning because writing is not fun for her. She lacks self-confidence in her writing ability. She is in 3rd grade this year and by next year she will have to be writing so much more.

What is your daughter most interested in? I expect that would propel her forward ....as Sue just said.

Another thought .....my youngest daughter always got really, really bad reports about her hand writing. She would always come home crying because her teaches said she wasn't trying hard enough. The poor little thing would just be sobbing and saying "Mommy - I am trying so hard"

It turns out a Doctor said she may have arthritis in her fingers ....This is so many years later so when I think about it seems a little odd but obviously her fingers were stiff for some reason.

She now has children of her own but her handwriting is still quite large and almost child like. Btw - she is a very bright young woman and her daughter has been identified as gifted.......so it is nothing to do with intelligence. She has a science degree ....and maybe more telling .....she is an incredible people person.

### Stardust — 2017-08-15T20:56:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221706>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221488)
>
> I am a grandmother .....my daughter loves cooking so she can read cookbooks and really connects with that.
>
> Actually - do you cook with your daughter? maybe reading a recipe and writing out in simple ingredient points would interest her?
>
> My younger daughter was a very difficult eater. We got her to chose a recipe and then help shop for stuff and then help make it.
>
> That made a huge difference - she then had control over the process.

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221572)
>
> What is your daughter most interested in? I expect that would propel her forward ....as Sue just said.
>
> Another thought .....my youngest daughter always got really, really bad reports about her hand writing. She would always come home crying because her teaches said she wasn't trying hard enough. The poor little thing would just be sobbing and saying "Mommy - I am trying so hard"
>
> It turns out a Doctor said she may have arthritis in her fingers ....This is so many years later so when I think about it seems a little odd but obviously her fingers were stiff for some reason.
>
> She now has children of her own but her handwriting is still quite large and almost child like. Btw - she is a very bright young woman and her daughter has been identified as gifted.......so it is nothing to do with intelligence. She has a science degree ....and maybe more telling .....she is an incredible people person.

I cook with her every so often. She loves SCIENCE! Last year it was apparent to her that her handwriting was dramatically different then her classmates. It weighed on her pretty bad. It was also mentioned at every intervention meeting we had with the school. She is very bright as well and often says she is trying her best .

### Stardust — 2017-08-15T21:02:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221707>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=221511)
>
> Its been a bumpy road since DS2 was a young child, light sensitive, food phobic, water phobic, anxious, delayed speech development ... (turned out he is incredibly sensitive to the effects of fluoride). Watching his inner doctor guide him to recovery when it got the chance is one of my greatest joys. A lot of moments of grace. We chose the guitar over the piano ... DS2 was keen to learn electric guitar, the lessons were geared mainly around acoustic but with enough electric to keep him motivated. Its turned out that he is good at it, but its not his passion, even the electric guitar. For lessons, he would do just enough practice, but if he'd done more because he was passionate about it, he would be exceptional. He sometimes goes outside and sits under the trees with his acoustic guitar, and every time he does I have another moment of grace. .

Isn't is amazing to watch recovering right in front of you?! My daughter has come a long way as well. Passion can lead to amazing things. I suspect she will like piano but it might not be her passion either lol. She loves sewing, science, math and roaming my parents 5 acres. Can't say I blame her.

### caroline — 2017-08-16T06:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221750>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221706)
>
> I cook with her every so often. She loves SCIENCE! Last year it was apparent to her that her handwriting was dramatically different then her classmates. It weighed on her pretty bad. It was also mentioned at every intervention meeting we had with the school. She is very bright as well and often says she is trying her best .

When it comes right down it it ....does it really matter what her handwriting looks like? Obviously she has many skills and interests.

Think about it .....you said she loves sewing- so her motor skills are good enuf for that? It seems odd that her hand writing is less than ideal?

Maybe something happened earlier on at her school and she was made to feel very self conscious about her writing????

Maybe her teachers should lay off - and applaud the things she is great at?!!

What instrument would she prefer? Have you thought of a ukulele? they are pretty easy to play when just starting and may give her a sense of accomplishment.......

### caroline — 2017-08-16T06:19:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221751>

One other thought ......bread making is quite therapeutic. All the kneading may give you some insight into how agile her hands/fingers are?

What could be more rewarding than baking a loaf of bread!

The more I think about it ....why does it matter so much about her handwriting. Maybe she should become a Doctor!

I bet Jack would have some interesting insight into this......

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-16T12:30:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221756>

> [Stardust said:](/goto/post?id=221707)
>
> Isn't is amazing to watch recovering right in front of you?! My daughter has come a long way as well. Passion can lead to amazing things. I suspect she will like piano but it might not be her passion either lol. She loves sewing, science, math and roaming my parents 5 acres. Can't say I blame her.

I did this with travel for 3 years when the kids were going to miss school (which at 6 and 4 wasn't such an issue with the school, but it helped the parent/teacher relationship that we weren't ignoring their education while we were away). But we went shopping and they each picked a book to use as a holiday journal, so their choice of colour and pattern/pictures on the cover were their own. Each day they would write a few lines on what in that day had interested them. It became their holiday souvenir. If they were enjoying something and wanted to put it in their journal later they could choose a postcard, or they would draw a picture, stick in a bus ticket etc.... The books they had chosen, a pen, colouring stuff and a glue stick ...their own interests and imagination, and no pressure. But the more they were interested in something the more they would write .... Maybe your daughter could journal what she notices on her roams in your parents 5 acres? And/or one on other subjects she finds interesting, perhaps a journal for each? The trick is to let them write spontaneously, no worrying about their handwriting or spelling, only help if asked, and if there are days when they really don't feel like doing it, or don't want to write more than a line, don't worry about it.

### Stardust — 2017-08-20T20:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221969>

> Think about it .....you said she loves sewing- so her motor skills are good enuf for that? It seems odd that her hand writing is less than ideal?
>
> Maybe something happened earlier on at her school and she was made to feel very self conscious about her writing????
>
> Maybe her teachers should lay off - and applaud the things she is great at?!!
>
> What instrument would she prefer? Have you thought of a ukulele? they are pretty easy to play when just starting and may give her a sense of accomplishment.......

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=221751)
>
> One other thought ......bread making is quite therapeutic. All the kneading may give you some insight into how agile her hands/fingers are?
>
> What could be more rewarding than baking a loaf of bread!
>
> The more I think about it ....why does it matter so much about her handwriting. Maybe she should become a Doctor!
>
> I bet Jack would have some interesting insight into this......

She absolutely has many skills and interests. She actually is always working with slime, putty and playdough. Writing increases in 3rd grade so this will be an interesting year. I don't have to worry about teachers this year because I'm the teacher. I pulled her out to homeschool. We saw regression when we vaccinated her at 2.5 years old and California's SB277 forced vaccine law was a problem. She left the school system with insecurity about writing though. I want to build that confidence back up. Piano is her preferred instrument I never considered the ukulele. I will look into that. Thank you Caroline!

### Stardust — 2017-08-20T20:48:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-221971>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=221756)
>
> I did this with travel for 3 years when the kids were going to miss school (which at 6 and 4 wasn't such an issue with the school, but it helped the parent/teacher relationship that we weren't ignoring their education while we were away). But we went shopping and they each picked a book to use as a holiday journal, so their choice of colour and pattern/pictures on the cover were their own. Each day they would write a few lines on what in that day had interested them. It became their holiday souvenir. If they were enjoying something and wanted to put it in their journal later they could choose a postcard, or they would draw a picture, stick in a bus ticket etc.... The books they had chosen, a pen, colouring stuff and a glue stick ...their own interests and imagination, and no pressure. But the more they were interested in something the more they would write .... Maybe your daughter could journal what she notices on her roams in your parents 5 acres? And/or one on other subjects she finds interesting, perhaps a journal for each? The trick is to let them write spontaneously, no worrying about their handwriting or spelling, only help if asked, and if there are days when they really don't feel like doing it, or don't want to write more than a line, don't worry about it.

How awesome!! What a fun thing to do while traveling. We started a journal with an idea from a homeschooling workshop I went to. We basically write messages back and forth. She has loved it so far! I love the idea of journaling at my parents. She climbs trees, makes "survival" tools, talks to bugs, builds shelters and many more things out there. Running out of ideas to write about will not be possible. Sue, thank you for all the suggestions!

### Penny — 2017-08-20T21:20:48+0300

Running out of ideas to write about will not be possible. Sue, thank you for all the suggestions!

I love how Montessori does hand writing - they trace sandpaper letters glued onto a wooden block - then they are constantly pulling pegs from puzzles (3 finger grip) - then they have a moveable alphabet where they make their own words, then read them, then write them - they are writing cursive at the age of 3... it's amazing to see:
[http://www.infomontessori.com/language/handwriting-introduction.htm](http://www.infomontessori.com/language/handwriting-introduction.htm)

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-21T09:58:48+0300

Another thought is a special pen for journaling... I was watching a you tube video on making a slime pen using the inside of a biro through a plastic Walmart bought syringe, and then filling the syringe with coloured and glittery slime. My kids would have loved that ... I can feel a Nana moment coming on ......

Another thought is chalk ... writing in chalk on a driveway, a smooth rock, chalk board signs to go in her shelters .... If a child writes "smooth rock" on a rock, it relates the written pattern of words to the visual pattern of the object. Or that written symbol patterns are a meaningful communication .. This way and arrows ..... (Name)'s den .....

How about a map? - she can make a map of a section of the 5 acres, mark or draw in the trees or things of interest and then write in what it is .... oak tree, lots of x bugs/birds/flowers here .... favourite shelter ....

### EvaJoh — 2017-08-25T10:30:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/penmanship.19994/post-222221>

Wait a bit more. Don’t stop the [occupational therapy services](http://www.newmummycompany.ca/services/occupational-therapy/) in the middle of the course. If you haven’t found any improvement under the present program then it shows that you need more intense services not a discontinuation of services.

Make a conversation with your OT regarding your child’s disabilities, behavior, daily routine etc. They can reschedule the therapy programs for your child. It can cause if the OT doesn’t understand your child’s problem properly.

Consult yet another OT. She/he might understand your child’s problem.

# Thread: New Israeli podcast talking about kids.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids

### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-11T08:49:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221406>

YAY! A new podcast - my favourite!

### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-11T15:54:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221434>

This was a fabulous podcast. My kids now watch no TV and will never play with a tablet - whilst I'm still breathing.

I'm also homeschooling because I'm no way letting my kids sit in a blue lit classroom all day!

I thought the bit at the end was interesting talking about low dopamine people not understanding the science. I fear that they might but that they just don't care enough.

I am that 'crazy' person right now trying to share some of this information but people don't want to know, even if they believe it on some level.

### caroline — 2017-08-12T10:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221505>

great podcast - really straightforward and to the point. There is always so much more to learn.

yesterday my closest family said it to me straight in my face... and made fun of me
That was during the family dinner when they saw me for the first time with my blue blockers on .
My response was straight forward...
It's amazing how much ignorance
It amazes me !
That low dopamine in people can Harm sometimes others... only with words
but my light won't let it go through...

### caroline — 2017-08-12T13:21:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221507>

keep moving forward on your optimal journey ........it would be lovely to have the support of your family, but this is all about you right now.

Don't be deterred by anyone.

### Sue-UK — 2017-08-12T14:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-israeli-podcast-talking-about-kids.19989/post-221509>

I still get the odd bit of light hearted teasing, but it doesn't bother me if what they say has me laughing with them. I might say why I'm doing something if they are interested, but I don't try and justify myself, and I won't take any shit. I would tell someone if they were going to be unpleasant, change the subject. My DD has had conversations with her friends (usually after a glass or three or more....) about who has the craziest parent. Apparently I'm still the winner by a long shot .... I've told her the day I'm not top of the list is the day I know I'm not trying hard enough.

# Thread: Leptin and Sleep Apnea
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-and-sleep-apnea.19997/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jason Coates — 2017-08-11T15:22:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-and-sleep-apnea.19997/post-221430>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28796527?dopt=Abstract](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28796527?dopt=Abstract)

Experimental studies using animal models of a low leptin state such as leptin deficiency have shown that leptin regulates sleep architecture, upper airway patency, ventilatory function, and hypercapnic ventilatory response. However, findings from human studies do not consistently support the data from the animal models. The effect of leptin on the pathophysiology of obstructive sleep apnea is being investigated, but the results of studies have been confounded by its diurnal variation and the short-term effects of feeding, adiposity, age, and gender. Improved study design and methods of assessing functional leptin levels, specifically its central versus peripheral effects, will improve our understanding of the role of leptin in sleep apnea.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Taurine - Light - GABA efflux in the retina... this explains a lot:-)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2017-08-12T22:29:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221556>

**Effects of Taurine and Light on Retinal GABA Content and the Efflux of 14C-GABA and 14C-Aspartate from Frog Retina**

-   Authors
-   [Authors and affiliations](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51#authorsandaffiliations)

-   Jasmine E. Haroutounian
-   Andranik M. Petrosian

Chapter

-   [4 Citations](http://www.bookmetrix.com/detail/chapter/e74d6558-47d7-4dc4-ab79-261476dc1d91#citations)
-   [143 Downloads](http://www.bookmetrix.com/detail/chapter/e74d6558-47d7-4dc4-ab79-261476dc1d91#downloads)

**Summary**
GABA content of isolated, dark adapted frog retina was found to be 3.15 ± 0.28 mM. After 30 minutes of exposure to intense light (200 1x), retinal GABA levels increased about 70%. Interestingly, incubation of dark adapted retina for 30 minutes with medium containing 0.4 mM taurine also led to a 70% increase in GABA levels. Since the light-induced elevation in GABA content was reduced over 50% by a simultaneous injection of 0.02 mM strychinine, it is likely that the light-induced GABA change is partly mediated by the release of taurine from the retina seen after light exposure. However, incubation of isolated retina with medium containing increasing concentrations of taurine (1, 2 and 20 mM), caused a progressive rise in 14C-GABA efflux from retina that was preloaded with 2.2 μM GABA and exposed to dim light (0.05 1x). It was also shown that taurine (1 and 5 mM) dramatically reduced 14C-aspartate efflux from retina preloaded with radioactive aspartate and exposed to dim light conditions. By comparison, intense light stimulation (40 1x) reduced basal 14C-aspartate efflux while dark exposure increased 14C-aspartate loss from the isolated retina. We found that taurine depressed the b-wave signal of frog retina, with the maximum effect occurring at a concentration of 1 mM. Addition of strychnine (0.4 mM) reversed the taurine effect on the b-wave, indicating that taurine receptors must be present in the inner retina. By contrast, taurine (0.1 – 20 mM) had no effect on the P111 component of the ERG initiated by either aspartate or cobalt. However, taurine exerted a modest depressant activity on P111 initiated by glutamate. The significance of these data relative to the putative neurotransmitter function of taurine in the inner retina is discussed.
[https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-0117-0\_51](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51)

### Alex97232 — 2017-08-14T23:11:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221659>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=221556)
>
> **Effects of Taurine and Light on Retinal GABA Content and the Efflux of 14C-GABA and 14C-Aspartate from Frog Retina**
>
> -   HAuthors
> -   [Authors and affiliations](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51#authorsandaffiliations)
>

> -   [4 Citations](http://www.bookmetrix.com/detail/chapter/e74d6558-47d7-4dc4-ab79-261476dc1d91#citations)
> -   [143 Downloads](http://www.bookmetrix.com/detail/chapter/e74d6558-47d7-4dc4-ab79-261476dc1d91#downloads)
>
> **Summary**
> GABA content of isolated, dark adapted frog retina was found to be 3.15 ± 0.28 mM. After 30 minutes of exposure to intense light (200 1x), retinal GABA levels increased about 70%. Interestingly, incubation of dark adapted retina for 30 minutes with medium containing 0.4 mM taurine also led to a 70% increase in GABA levels. Since the light-induced elevation in GABA content was reduced over 50% by a simultaneous injection of 0.02 mM strychinine, it is likely that the light-induced GABA change is partly mediated by the release of taurine from the retina seen after light exposure. However, incubation of isolated retina with medium containing increasing concentrations of taurine (1, 2 and 20 mM), caused a progressive rise in 14C-GABA efflux from retina that was preloaded with 2.2 μM GABA and exposed to dim light (0.05 1x). It was also shown that taurine (1 and 5 mM) dramatically reduced 14C-aspartate efflux from retina preloaded with radioactive aspartate and exposed to dim light conditions. By comparison, intense light stimulation (40 1x) reduced basal 14C-aspartate efflux while dark exposure increased 14C-aspartate loss from the isolated retina. We found that taurine depressed the b-wave signal of frog retina, with the maximum effect occurring at a concentration of 1 mM. Addition of strychnine (0.4 mM) reversed the taurine effect on the b-wave, indicating that taurine receptors must be present in the inner retina. By contrast, taurine (0.1 – 20 mM) had no effect on the P111 component of the ERG initiated by either aspartate or cobalt. However, taurine exerted a modest depressant activity on P111 initiated by glutamate. The significance of these data relative to the putative neurotransmitter function of taurine in the inner retina is discussed.
> [https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-0117-0\_51](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007%2F978-1-4899-0117-0_51)

Thank you, Rabbit, for this and all of your posts. I know this is important, but not sure I follow: Can you translate this to non-geek? TIA.

### Da-mo — 2017-08-15T02:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221672>

Id like to know too - especially with many of the energy drinks these days containing Taurine.

> **Scientists close in on taurine's activity in the brain (Red Bull drinkers, take note)**
> Taurine is one of the most plentiful amino acids in the human brain, but neuroscientists are still puzzled by just how brain cells put it to use. Now, a team of researchers at Weill Cornell Medical College (WCMC) has uncovered a prime site of activity for the molecule, bringing them closer to solving that mystery.
> "We have discovered that taurine is a strong activator of what are known as GABA \[gamma-aminobutyric acid\] receptors in a regulatory area of the brain called the thalamus," said study senior author Neil L. Harrison, professor of pharmacology and pharmacology in anesthesiology at WCMC. "We had discovered these receptors two years ago and showed that they interact with the neurotransmitter GABA -- the brain's key inhibitory transmitter -- that is also involved in brain development. It seems that taurine shares these receptors."
> The finding, reported in the January issue of the Journal of Neuroscience_,_ opens the door to better understanding taurine's impact on the brain.
> While the amino acid is made naturally by the body, it's also a much-touted ingredient in such so-called "energy drinks" as Red Bull. "Its inclusion in these supplements is a little puzzling, because our research would suggest ... \[that\] taurine actually would have more of a sedative effect on the brain," Harrison said.
> The prime focus of the study, however, was to find a site for the neurological activity of taurine.
> "Scientists have long questioned whether taurine might act on an as-yet-undiscovered receptor of its own," said lead researcher Fan Jia, postdoctoral scientist in anesthesiology. "But after some recent work in our lab, we ended up zeroing in on this population of GABA receptors in the thalamus."
> The thalamus, located deep in the brain's center, is involved in what neuroscientists call "behavioral state control," helping to regulate transitions between sleep and wakefulness, for example.
> "It's like a railway junction, controlling information traffic between the brainstem, the senses and the executive functions in the cortex," Harrison said. The researchers exposed thin slices of thalamic tissue from the brains of mice to concentrations of taurine that were similar to what might be found in the human brain.
> "We found that taurine is extraordinarily active on this population of GABA receptors in the thalamus," Harrison said. "It came as a bit of a surprise that the same receptor was used by both taurine and GABA. Nevertheless, finding taurine's receptor has been like discovering the 'missing link' in taurine biology."
> Now, Harrison's group is trying to determine what taurine actually does in the brain; Harrison said that because GABA is important for forging new cell-to-cell connections in the developing brain, "and because taurine shares a receptor with GABA, it, too, may play a role in neurological development."
> As for energy drinks, "Remarkably little is known about the effects of energy drinks on the brain," Harrison said. "Assuming that some of \[taurine\] does get absorbed, the taurine ... may actually play a role in the crash people often report after drinking these highly caffeinated beverages."
> The work was funded by the U.S. National Institutes of Health. Co-researchers include Minerva Yue, Angelo Keramidas and Peter A. Goldstein of WCMC as well as Dev Chandra and Gregg E. Homanics of the University of Pittsburgh.

[https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2008/02/scientists-close-taurines-activity-brain](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2008/02/scientists-close-taurines-activity-brain)

### Da-mo — 2017-08-15T02:56:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221673>

> **Effect of taurine and caffeine on sleep–wake activity in _Drosophila melanogaster_**
> [Fang Ju Lin](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lin%20FJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 [Michael M Pierce](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Pierce%20MM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 [Amita Sehgal](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Sehgal%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),2 [Tianyi Wu](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Wu%20T%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 [Daniel C Skipper](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Skipper%20DC%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711),1 and [Radhika Chabba](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chabba%20R%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23616711)1
> 1Department of Biology, Coastal Carolina University, Conway, SC, USA
> 2Department of Neuroscience, HHMI at University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA, USA
> Correspondence: Fang Ju Lin, Department of Biology, Coastal Carolina University, P.O. Box 261954, Conway, SC 29528, USA Tel +1 843 349 6484 Fax +1 843 349 2201 Email [Author information ► Copyright and License information ►](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#)
> [Copyright](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/about/copyright/) © 2010 Lin et al, publisher and licensee Dove Medical Press Ltd
> This is an Open Access article which permits unrestricted noncommercial use, provided the original work is properly cited.
> This article has been [cited by](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/citedby/) other articles in PMC.
> [Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#)
> **Abstract**
> Caffeine and taurine are two major neuromodulators present in large quantities in many popular energy drinks. We investigated their effects on sleep-wake control in constant darkness using the fruit fly _Drosophila_ as a model system. It has been shown that caffeine, as the most widely used psychostimulant, can boost arousal through the dopamine pathway in the mushroom bodies of flies. Taurine is a GABA receptor agonist, which is inhibitory to neuronal firing. We show here that flies receiving a low dose of caffeine (0.01%) increase locomotor activity by 25%, and decrease total sleep by 15%. Treatment with taurine at 0.1% to 1.5% reduces locomotor activity by 28% to 86%, and shifts it from diurnal to nocturnal. At 0.75%, taurine also increases total sleep by 50%. Our results show that taurine increases sleep, while caffeine, as previously reported, attenuates sleep. Flies treated with both caffeine and taurine exhibit two differential effects which depend upon the ratio of taurine to caffeine. A high taurine:caffeine ratio promotes sleep, while a low ratio of taurine:caffeine inhibits sleep to a greater extent than the equivalent amount of caffeine alone. This intriguing enhancement of caffeine action by low doses of taurine may account for the presence of both compounds in energy-promoting drinks such as Red Bull® and Monster®.
> **Keywords:** caffeine (trimethylxanthine), energy drinks, GABA (γ-aminobutyric acid), locomotor activity, sleep, taurine (2-aminoethanesulfonic acid)
> In the last decade, energy drinks such as Red Bull® and Monster® have become cultural icons and are frequently marketed towards and consumed by young adults. These drinks are intended to keep people awake at work or during leisure activities. Unfortunately the frequent consumption of these beverages has been linked to risky behavior among college students.[1](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b1-nss-2-221) Two common ingredients found in energy drinks are caffeine and taurine, both classified as neuromodulators. The lack of regulation of caffeine content that could lead to caffeine intoxication, and its recreational use in combination with alcohol, have become growing concerns (see review by Reissig).[2](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b2-nss-2-221) According to US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) regulations, the maximum caffeine content allowed is 71 mg/12 fl.oz. (or 0.02%),[3](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b3-nss-2-221) yet many energy drinks have clearly exceeded that limit for a single serving without any warning label. The amount of caffeine per serving in energy drinks ranges from 76–80 mg or 0.03% (eg, original Red Bull® or Monster®), equivalent to that in a single cup of coffee, to 280 mg or 0.11% (eg, Cocaine®) in 8.3 oz. or 250 ml. Monster XXL® has as much as 240 mg of caffeine in a 23.5 oz. can. The side effects of caffeine include insomnia, nausea, heart palpitations, and even death in rare cases. Caffeine is also known to delay the onset of sleep, shorten stage 3–4 nonrapid eye movement (NREM) sleep and reduce total sleep.[4](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b4-nss-2-221) In addition, taurine, a sulfhydryl amino acid found in many tissues including the CNS and muscles, is involved in osmotic regulation[5](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b5-nss-2-221) and in modulating the release of other amino acid transmitters at synapses.[6](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b6-nss-2-221) Reported benefits of taurine include cytoprotection[7](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b7-nss-2-221) and anti-seizure/epilepsy effects.[8](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b8-nss-2-221) Both Red Bull® and Monster® have 1000 mg or 0.4% of taurine in the original 250 ml packages, and the amount triples in Monster XXL®. Though manufacturers claim that caffeine and taurine are safe and beneficial, the interaction between caffeine and taurine has not fully been investigated.
> The fruit fly _Drosophila melanogaster_ serves as a useful model to study the sleep-wake cycle since it shares conserved mechanisms with mammals.[9](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b9-nss-2-221)–[11](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b11-nss-2-221) The circadian (~24 hour) clock that maintains the timing of the sleep-wake cycle in _Drosophila_ resides in the lateral neurons (LNs) of the brain. Within these neurons, a molecular clock maintained by the products of key clock genes: _period_ (_per_), _timeless_ (_tim_), _Clock_ (_Clk_), and _cycle_ (_cyc_), results in the rhythmic regulation of a neuropeptide, PDF (pigment-dispersing factor).[12](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b12-nss-2-221) In turn, PDF promotes arousal during the late night/early morning via PDF-responsive neurons that control downstream motor activity. A second process controlling the sleep-wake cycle, also known as homeostatic control of sleep, determines the amount of sleep rather than the timing of sleep. Results from the Hendricks and Shaw groups have demonstrated that the rest behavior _of Drosophila_ is independent of the circadian clock, and it exhibits similar characteristics to mammalian sleep behavior.[13](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b13-nss-2-221),[14](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b14-nss-2-221) Mushroom bodies (MBs), though best known for their function in learning and memory, are also involved in suppressing locomotion,[15](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b15-nss-2-221) and promoting sleep[16](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b16-nss-2-221),[17](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b17-nss-2-221) at least in part through serotonin signaling.[18](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b18-nss-2-221)
> Neurotransmitters such as dopamine, glutamate, norepinephrine, orexin, and serotonin are involved in promoting wakefulness in mammals,[19](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b19-nss-2-221) while adenosine along with other neuropeptides modulate sleep.[20](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b20-nss-2-221),[21](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b19-nss-2-221) Similar pathways are present in _Drosophila_, with arousal controlled by dopamine and octopamine,[22](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b22-nss-2-221),[23](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b23-nss-2-221) while sleep is promoted by the GABAergic pathway and the serotonin d5-HT1A receptor.[18](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b18-nss-2-221) GABA elicits a Cl− current and hyperpolarizes the cell membrane, hence it is generally considered to be an inhibitory neurotransmitter in _Drosophila_,[24](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b24-nss-2-221) although excitatory GABA receptors have also been identified in invertebrates.[25](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b25-nss-2-221),[26](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b26-nss-2-221) Similarly, it has been shown that taurine is a GABA/glycine receptor agonist,[27](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b27-nss-2-221)–[29](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b29-nss-2-221) though it requires much higher concentration. Agosto[30](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b30-nss-2-221) and Parisky[31](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/#b31-nss-2-221) were the first to link sleep behavior to GABA receptors in _Drosophila_, which were identified in the lateral neurons (LNs). Our specific aims in this study were to: 1) study the effect of taurine on sleep; and 2) examine the caffeine:taurine interaction involved in sleep–wake cycle control, at the same ratio found in the energy drinks.

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3630960/)

### Da-mo — 2017-08-15T03:01:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/taurine-light-gaba-efflux-in-the-retina-this-explains-a-lot.20002/post-221674>

> **GABA & Glycine Receptor Modulation**
> While taurine fails to easily cross the blood-brain barrier, **_the compound engages a unique carrier-mediated transport system that delivers it across the blood-retinal barrier, essentially giving taurine backdoor access to nervous tissue._** Once there, taurine functions as an inhibitory neurotransmitter, calming brain activity by engaging the GABA & glycine receptors. Modulation of these brain systems reduces excess stimulation (THINK: anxiety & stress) while exerting anxiolytic relaxation.
> **Anti-Aging Neuroprotection**
> Taurine is often touted as an antioxidant, occasionally as an anti-inflammatory. This [study](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23963537) suggests that taurine’s neuroprotective role is an amalgamation of effects, particularly on the GABAergic & somatostatinergic (growth hormone inhibitor) system, with taurine altering measures of GABA & glutamate, neuropeptide somatostatin, hippocampal expression, and more. Through interaction with the GABAergic system, taurine supplementation is believed to help forestall age-related cognitive decline.

[http://nootropicgeek.com/taurine-review/](http://nootropicgeek.com/taurine-review/)

# Thread: Balance and Co-ordination
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/balance-and-co-ordination.20008/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sajid Mahmood — 2017-08-14T12:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/balance-and-co-ordination.20008/post-221638>

My 5 years 8 months old son struggles with his balance and co-ordination. He has been having balance problems since he was about he has been 18 months old. When he was 2 years, he used to fall over quite easily when running. Other toddlers similar age never used to fall like him. Now that he is 5 years of age, he still struggles with his balance. As soon as I tell him to stand on one leg he struggles to hold it, and wobbles all over the place and falls over. When we went on holiday to Pakistan at the 33rd Latitude, his symptoms seemed to improve. I am developing him to become a soccer player, therefore have been teaching him to play football since he was 1 years of age. He has been also going to swimming, boxing, gymnastics. We also make him do plenty of bodyweight exercises at home. So I doubt very much he has a problem with his motor skills.

At the time, when my wife was pregnant, the mid-wife nurses stopped my wife from having fish and seafood due to the mercury toxicity. Little did I know at the time how important DHA was. Also when my wife was pregnant she was in a blue lit environment filled with nnEMF. I wish I had stumbled on Jack's work at that time. My wife is also Pakistani descent but she was pregnant in UK, 53rd Latitude. Moreover, when my son was born he was born in a blue light environment, filled with nnEMF.

Also not to mention his attention span is bad when he goes to classes, and is not very good at listening or following instructions. A lot of the time he's daydreaming or having difficulty following instructions and doesn't pay attention to details. Is there a chance he could have mild/moderate autism or ADHD? Also he tends to be clumsy and careless when running, thus falls over a lot and bumps into things. He's usually not looking where he is going? When we went to Pakistan he seemed to become a lot sharper mentally, with his listening, and with following instructions. In Pakistan at the 33rd Latitude there was obviously a much stronger UV environment and was much less nnEMF. In Pakistan we live in a village, surrounded by trees and greenery and the electricity turns off every hour or so.

Could the problem be because of nnEMF and blue light? What things could I do improve his balance and co-ordination? Will a blacklight help?

### Sheddie — 2017-08-14T15:31:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/balance-and-co-ordination.20008/post-221640>

**_"What things could I do improve his balance and co-ordination? "_**

Maybe his "motor skills" need 'reset exercises' before physical training classes IF those classes aren't incorporating something to strengthen both halves of the developing brain, like in the warm-ups -- before more rigorous and focused physical and/or mental practices?

There was an older book I liked but I can't find at the moment and forget the title/author (low dopa for decades!). It was material to do with readying/settling little kids before they sit down and 'learn.' I remember some moves like "zip-ups" and performing standing motions like doing a dry-land swim crawl. (Too bad I never thought of doing these with my ADD kid under the morning outdoor sky, neither see them in action except under schoolroom lighting.)

All I could find quickly was something like, [https://www.brainbalancecenters.com...-exercises-promote-hemispheric-brain-balance/](https://www.brainbalancecenters.com/blog/2014/08/brain-boot-camp-home-exercises-promote-hemispheric-brain-balance/) -- but you get the idea and could find something fun and doable.

What I'm correlating here with this, is The Revolutionary Idea that recently made instant sense -- if I'm not synched with the solar cycle nothing else I do to ameliorate my health is going to be corrective or reverse burdensome symptoms. Of course, there's more, but that's what blew me away.)

Addendum: I might have missed it, but I assume you've ruled out brain inflammation which, I think, has validity in some of these diagnoses that seem epidemic (epi-genetic?) in kids these days, and, the usual developmental assessments of hearing, vision and so on?

### Ted — 2017-08-18T01:28:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/abscessed-molar.20014/post-221845>

I would listen to your dentist if you have one that you trust. Infections can spread and can be life-threatening.

### Rubicon — 2017-08-18T02:57:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/abscessed-molar.20014/post-221848>

If the tooth can't be saved, personally I'd avoid a root canal and go right to extraction. There are potential long-term risks of chronic infection with root canals.

I was thinking same.... It is wisdom one... so should be no problem

# Thread: Nightmares?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nightmares.20015/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-16T14:45:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nightmares.20015/post-221760>

Has anyone any insights on nightmares?

Both my husband and I have been experiencing one nightly.

We removed mouldy mattresses a few weeks ago. I'm wondering if it's connected.

We've been following the protocols. Could this be some kind of detox?

It wakes me up and disturbs my sleep...

Any thoughts welcome!

### Allin — 2017-08-17T02:10:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nightmares.20015/post-221791>

Nightmares are the reflection of the stresses in your daily life. Different situations which are going on with your kids, job, news you hear or the TV you watch, the different discussion groups you participate in help create the nightmare. What is your mind set? Do you have nightmares concerning areas where you are winning, taking control or improving the situation? No you don't. No one does. Change your mind set where you use those nightmares to high-light or focus you attention. _"Nightmares - Another Great Tool for Self Awareness"_

### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-17T08:57:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nightmares.20015/post-221799>

> [Allin said:](/goto/post?id=221791)
>
> Nightmares are the reflection of the stresses in your daily life. Different situations which are going on with your kids, job, news you hear or the TV you watch, the different discussion groups you participate in help create the nightmare. What is your mind set? Do you have nightmares concerning areas where you are winning, taking control or improving the situation? No you don't. No one does. Change your mind set where you use those nightmares to high-light or focus you attention. _"Nightmares - Another Great Tool for Self Awareness"_

I appreciate your input.

I don't suppose I see myself as very stressed - apart from taking care of my two kids. And I actively avoid TV and the news, the only scary things I see are the things posted on this forum haha!

It's possible of course. It's interesting that my husband is also experiencing them.

### Allin — 2017-08-17T09:12:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nightmares.20015/post-221800>

Five years ago I would have suggested maybe it was something you and your husband ate... nnEMF was not in my awareness at that time, neither sunlight nor magnetism either.

### ScottishEmma — 2017-08-17T09:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nightmares.20015/post-221801>

> [Allin said:](/goto/post?id=221800)
>
> Five years ago I would have suggested maybe it was something you and your husband ate... nnEMF was not in my awareness at that time, neither sunlight nor magnetism either.

Yes, that crossed my mind too but I can't find a single thing we've both eaten consistently. But you're right, I'll start a food journal and see.

### Sheddie — 2017-08-19T15:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mainstream-media-catching-on-to-dr-kruse-teachings.20025/post-221937>

> [Brent Patrick said:](/goto/post?id=221856)
>
> [View attachment 5526](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/5526/) Daily Telegraph Sydney Australia 18/08/2017
>
> Algae = DHA major source in life. DHA is the major biophysical chemical that transfers energy , information, momentum from one biome to another. It can do the same from one form of life to another. 600 million years ago oxygen and DHA showed up at the same time in evolutionary history giving algae the ability to know how to interact with beneficial microbes while it was still in the water.
> **[Dr. Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/?hc_ref=ARRLy1QhlVmnK2BJlbbap9LwXNQCkQuGQc3Jv5wr0hpLEj8H0BhvHOeen-8f2Uz9ukw&fref=nf) FB**
> · [October 6, 2015](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/posts/1011683788895975)

There was a book by Thomas Gold about oil & gas being made by bacteria that live and migrate in rock, and thus, a self-renewing resource; his multi-disciplinary critical thinking countered global public relations media at the time about scarcity of oil on Earth (that oil reserves are finite). Googling him today, it seems he has been marginalized over time, but I do remember the book was a very interesting read. I'm not sure the article above (@Brent) is a directly connectable phenom but the oil-from-rock picture made Gold's thesis pop up. I do remember being interested that Gold thought there was Helium in the earth's crust -- another item of 'cognitive dissonance' I never resolved...

\[from Wikipedia\]
**The Deep Hot Biosphere\[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gold&action=edit&section=9)\]**
In a 1992 paper "The Deep Hot Biosphere" in the _[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proceedings_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences)_,[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Gold#cite_note-55) Gold first suggested that microbial life is widespread in the porosity of the crust of the Earth, down to depths of several kilometers, where rising temperatures finally set a limit. The subsurface life obtains its energy not from photosynthesis but from chemical sources in fluids migrating upwards through the crust. The mass of the deep biosphere may be comparable to that of the surface biosphere. Subsurface life may be widespread on other bodies in the solar system and throughout the universe, even on worlds unaccompanied by other stars.

Gold also published a book of the same title in 1999, which expanded on the arguments in his 1992 paper and included speculations on the [origin of life](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Origin_of_life).[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Gold#cite_note-56)

According to Gold, bacteria feeding on the oil accounts for the presence of biological debris in [hydrocarbon fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fossil_fuel), obviating the need to resort to a [biogenic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biogenic) theory for the origin of the latter. The flows of underground [hydrocarbons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrocarbon) may also explain oddities in the concentration of other mineral deposits.

In short, Gold said about the origin of natural hydrocarbons (petroleum and natural gas): _Hydrocarbons are not biology reworked by geology (as the traditional view would hold), but rather geology reworked by biology_.[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Gold#cite_note-MetaResearch-42)

# Thread: Skin in the game - Help with PCOS, Hashis and creativity!  Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-in-the-game-help-with-pcos-hashis-and-creativity-journal.20027/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9

### KruseKadet — 2017-08-18T15:01:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-in-the-game-help-with-pcos-hashis-and-creativity-journal.20027/post-221878>

Hey Kruse-Folk,
Would love some help with my PCOS, Hashis, obesity and hormone mega-fail. I want to be a bright shiny peep, not a dreggy dopamine-vortex where creativity goes to die!! Would so appreciate help as am new to science!

Had a quick one-on-one with Dr Kruse. He thought my labs were alarming, and possibly incorrect, re my super high oestrogen. He told me to change my environment, and get retested, which I am in the process of doing. Currently investigating EMF proofing, and getting 3 hrs am sunlight, fish meal each morning, grounding, icewater face dipping, and trying to avoid carbohydrates. I am failing on the latter. Still drawn obsessively back to sugar just when I think I have broken it’s back, so it’s clear my dopamine / beta endorphin production is not yet fully cooking. Despite BAB breaky, mood falls low at 4pm, and craving. This seems crucial to me.

Is there anything else I can do to help support the changes to happen? I am on no thyroid meds, nor hormone assist at all. Australia is a wasteland for red-pilled doctors, zero help. Would progesterone or DHEA help me? Was put on it once before by a doc years ago and it seemed to help…

If any other women are working these angles, PCOS, HASHIS etc, would love to hear from you, and share the progress.

Much love to Dr Kruse for rocking our collective reality! X Kruse Kadet

HISTORY
vegetarian from age 6.
baby sat by TV from early age.
sugar binging from age 7
miopia age 8
zero sun ever.
yoyo dieting starving - sugar binging - obesity.
glasses and contact lenses constantly.
low DHEA, chronic anaemia, hypoglycaemia - diagnosed aged 22
musician, working in studio is my job, blue light toxic for decades.
depression - obsessive and negative thinking,
night owl. 3am often the sleep time.
No sweating and repulsion to cold.
fried ovaries with laptop for decades.
zero fish ever.
obsessive worker till adrenal fatigue blow out. Now lacking drive for passion projects.
low dopamine relationships (no more!)
fibroids in uterus seen in ultrasound four years ago.
periods stopped coming, huge build up of material but nothing coming out for a year and a half. Very uncomfortable, till I blasted them back to working with iodine painting.

Parents.
Obese but very lovely mother, athlete father, both older at conception.
Mother arthritis and macular degeneration. Father faulty heart valve, glaucoma, died of an aneurysm after triple bypass surgery.

CURRENT LABS

IODINE URINE corrected - 51.0 - (when range for deficiency is 51>100)
CREATININE Urine Spot - 4.9L - (when normal range between 5.0 >13.0 mmol)

25 OH VITAMIN D
Result 76 - (Normal Range in Australia is between 50>250m IU/l)
\- I got this up from 50 in just 4 months!

THYROID FUNCTION ASSESSMENT
TSH 2.15 - (Normal Range 0.50 - 5.00 mIU/L)
FREE T4 14.1 - (Normal Range - 11.0 - 21.0 pmol/L )
FREE T3 4.5 - (Normal Range 3.1 - 6.0 pmol/L )
Reverse T3 200.0 - (Normal Range \*L 230.0 - 540.0 pmol/L )
FT3 : Reverse T3 Ratio ( X 100) 2.235 - (Normal Range \*H 1.200 - 2.200)

THYROID AUTO-Abs
THYROID PEROXIDASE Ab. 122 - (Normal Range \*H 0.0 - 35.0 IU/mL )
ANTITHYROGLOBULIN Ab. 56.3 - (Normal Range 0.0 - 115 IU/mL )
TSH RECEPTOR AB <1.0 - (Normal Range 0.0 - 1.5)

HORMONES

Progesterone Metabolites
b-Pregnanediol - Below premenopausal range - 435.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 450 - 2300)
a-Pregnanediol - Below premenopausal range - 90.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 120 - 740)

Androgen Metabolites
DHEAS - Low end of range - 68.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 23 - 350)
Androsterone - Within range - 1282.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 400 - 1700)
Etiocholanolone - High end of range- 855.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 260 - 950)
Testosterone - Within range - 6.1 ng/mg - (Normal Range 4 - 14)
5a-DHT - High end of range - 7.2 ng/mg - (Normal Range 0 - 8.8)
5a-Androstanediol - Within range -18.6 ng/mg - (Normal Range 12 - 30)
5b-Androstanediol - Above range - 145.4 ng/mg - (Normal Range 20 - 75)
Epi-Testosterone - Low end of range - 5.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 4.5 - 22.3)

Estrogen Metabolites
Estrone(E1) - Above range - 60.4 ng/mg - (Normal Range 12 - 26\_
Estradiol(E2) - Above range - 15.6 ng/mg - (Normal Range 1.8 - 4.5)
Estriol(E3) - Above range - 30.8 ng/mg - (Normal Range 5 - 18)
2-OH-E1 - Above range - 19.0 ng/mg - (Normal Range 4.6 - 14.4)
4-OH-E1 - Above range - 2.7 ng/mg (Normal Range 0 - 1.8)
16-OH-E1 - Above range - 4.6 ng/mg - (Normal Range 1 - 3.5)

# Thread: MCT oil and foods
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-foods.20037/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Billybats — 2017-08-21T15:13:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-foods.20037/post-221995>

Hello All,

Can one get MCT from just regular food. Is there an extra nutrient/mineral/fat in MCT oil that you can't get from food. Also Dr. Kruse says we are bacteria, (this may be a dumb thought) then why would one take coconut oil and/or MCT oil if they are antibacteria, antifungal, and antiviral. I could see for the antiviral and maybe the fungal, unless by taking them it balances it out and that may be why some people have reactions and some none. Maybe more helpful as antiviral since they say viruses are the cause of diseases, or not, unless I am understanding viruses wrong or got that all wrong. Thinking out loud.

Thank you

### WalterNL — 2017-08-21T20:53:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-foods.20037/post-222006>

MCT oil is partly caprylic and partly capric acid, these are saturad fatty acids that can be found in many foods. Coconuts, palm oil, butter, other dairy products, etc. There is nothing special in MCT oil, it is merely a refined version of coconut oil (keep in mind that coconut oil is a refined product by itself). The antiviral/antibacterial properties are mostly related to lauric acid, which is not in most MCT oils, but it is in the natural package. It has been shown to kill several harmful germs, but it definitely does not kill ALL bacteria/funghi/viruses.

### Billybats — 2017-08-21T21:09:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-and-foods.20037/post-222008>

Thank you WalterNL. I wasn't sure but that was pretty close to what I was thinking. Another reason I pretty much stopped all supplements except turmeric. I still have coconut oil but may just use it on my hair now. When I took multivitamins and other supplements they messed me up. This site has completed the puzzle of mine.

> MB has a low toxicity and is used for staining cells in vivo \[1, 17\]. In aqueous solutions it is in monomer and dimmer forms and **has two peaks of absorption at 668 and 609 nm.** MB can be activated by irradiation with appropriate wavelength in the red spectral range to an excited state, which in turn activates oxygen to yield oxidising radicals. Such radicals have the phototoxic effect on pathological cells, bacteria and viruses \[17-23\].

Did you know you're a prophet [https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2020/12/201204155410.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2020/12/201204155410.htm)
_
In the new test, the Cas13 protein is combined with a reporter molecule that becomes fluorescent when cut, and then mixed with a patient sample from a nasal swab. The sample is placed in a device that attaches to a smartphone. If the sample contains RNA from SARS-CoV-2, Cas13 will be activated and will cut the reporter molecule, causing the emission of a fluorescent signal. Then, the smartphone camera, essentially converted into a microscope, can detect the fluorescence and report that a swab tested positive for the virus._

# Thread: Why people are so much concerned about weight loss?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-people-are-so-much-concerned-about-weight-loss.20053/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jay Hall — 2017-08-24T20:17:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-people-are-so-much-concerned-about-weight-loss.20053/post-222188>

People want to lase weight and they work on it for a good reason! Obesity has turning out as a major risk factor for heart strokes, high BP, heart disease, diabetes and cancer. All physical activity seems to be more difficult when you need to carry around a great amount of additional weight. moreover, Extra weight can also be the reason for different sleep related problems. When you are quite weight and lay down the weight pressure of the weight can make breathing difficult. Even turning over in bed is hard when you are very big.

Losing weight demands a considerable amount of consideration and motivation. Eating habits are mainly hard, as you need to make choices on a daily basis and many times a day. It’s quite different from quitting smoking or drinking. With nearly any other abusable material, you can just quit using entirely. This cannot be the case with food. You should manage it well.

Many people use _[natural appetite suppressants for weight loss](http://www.polestarnutraceuticals.com/product/brainikus-diet-drops-with-african-mango/),_ which also is a good option for the purpose yet the consumer should also be motivated to quit bad eating habits.

### Lahelada — 2017-08-24T20:21:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-people-are-so-much-concerned-about-weight-loss.20053/post-222191>

Spam has too much salt for my bp !!

# Thread: Trad. diet of Indigenous Tribes of Sydney Australia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trad-diet-of-indigenous-tribes-of-sydney-australia.20059/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KruseKadet — 2017-08-26T03:55:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trad-diet-of-indigenous-tribes-of-sydney-australia.20059/post-222270>

Hey Folks,
This is a thread for research on what the first nation peoples of Sydney Australia, my own region, ate. I'm curious to know the foods that sustained tribes in this region reasonably unchanged for thousands of years. If it works for them, it must make sense for us!

Sydney is at latitude -33
The following is an article on the Indigenous epi-paleo diet by Anthropologist Dr Val Attenbrow of the Australian Museum website.
[https://australianmuseum.net.au/fish-maugro](https://australianmuseum.net.au/fish-maugro)
Articles on plant-based foods to come!

**Fish, _maugro_**
The Aboriginal people of coastal Sydney had their own names for the different species of fish, some of which were recorded by First Fleet officers. The general name for fish was _maugro_.

[](https://australianmuseum.net.au/image/Snapper-head-bones/)
Snapper head bones
Photographer: © Australian Museum

Only two of the fish seen caught or eaten were named by the British colonists - these were bream and mullet. Colonists noticed that people did not appear to like eating stingrays and sharks, though these fish may have been eaten at times when other fish were in short supply.

The people around Sydney Harbour had their own names for the different species of fish, some of which were recorded by First Fleet officers. The general name for fish was _maugro_.

-   **Karóoma** - black bream (probably Yellow-Finned Bream, Acanthropagrus australis)
-   **Caroom-a** - black fish (probably Yellow-Finned Bream, Acanthropagrus australis)
-   **Waaragal** - mackerel (possibly Blue Mackeral, Scomber australasicus)
-   **Ca-gone** - 'toad fish which they say is poisonous'
-   **Bado-berong** - 'a small fish like a tadpole with two feet'
-   **Murray-naugul** - flathead
-   **Cow-er-re** - large flat head
-   **Mul-lin-a-gul** - the small flat head
-   **Wal-lu-mai** - snapper
-   **Wa-ra-diel** - mullet large sort
-   **Ma-ro-me-ra** - the Zebra fish
-   **Ba-rong** - the prince fish
-   **Go-ray Ta-ra-wine** - unidentified, but possibly Tarwhine Rhabdosargus sarba
-   **Beragallon** - unidentified
-   **Moo-row-ul** - unidentified
-   **Boorroo-naga-naga** - unidentified
-   **Wallo-mill** - the Bullheaded Shark
-   **Gin-nare** - the shovel-nosed ray without a sting
-   **Te-ring-yan** - another sting-ray

The archaeological record shows a variety of fish were eaten in the coastal zone. Snapper and bream are the most commonly identified fish remains in coastal middens, though mullet, flathead, groper, morwong, Tarwhine and leatherjacket are also present as well as, Yellowtail Kingfish, Australian Salmon, Trevally, Luderick, Wrasse/Parrot fish, Whiting, Flounder, Catfish, Mulloway, Wirrah and Rock Cod.The archaeological evidence from Sydney Harbour and its tributaries suggests that less fish were eaten along the estuarine reaches of its tributaries than near the Harbour mouth.

Watkin Tench recorded the following method of preparing fish:

'_by throwing the fish, exactly in the state in which it came from the water, on the fire. When it has become a little warmed they take it off, rub away the scales, and then peal off with their teeth the surface, which they find done, and eat. Now, and not before, they gut it; but if the fish be a mullet, or any other which has a fatty substance about the intestines, they carefully guard that part, and esteem it a delicacy. The cooking is now completed, by the remaining part being laid on the fire until it be sufficiently done_' (Tench 1793:196).

The only fish the colonists noticed Aboriginal people eating along the Nepean-Hawkesbury River was mullet although many other species inhabit that river.

[Dr Val Attenbrow](https://australianmuseum.net.au/staff/val-attenbrow) , Principal Research Scientist
Last Updated: 24 December 2009
